text: Hindsight
To play it safe
A leap of faith
A fork in the road
To second-guess yourself
A missed opportunity
On a whim
To make the most of
To look back on
To have second thoughts
To regret
To talk yourself out of
If I had said no, I would have missed everything.
If I hadn't gone, I would never have met them.
I wish I had started sooner.
If only I hadn't been so careful.
You've cycled all over, Albania, Morocco, Yemen. Was there ever a trip you almost didn't take?
Albania, actually. In hindsight, I nearly talked myself out of it. If I hadn't gone, I would have missed the best trip of my life.
It's funny, isn't it? We stand at a fork in the road and the safe path looks so tempting. I played it safe once, and I still regret it.
What was the road you didn't take?
A job abroad, when I was thirty. I had second thoughts, and I said no. If I had been braver, my whole life would have been different.
I know that feeling. These days I try to make the most of every chance. If only we could tell our younger selves to be less careful.
In hindsight, I should have said yes.
If I had known, I would have gone.
If only I hadn't played it safe.
I don't regret it for a second.

Marcelo, it's Greta. I have been thinking about our conversation on the trail, about the roads not taken. You know, when I was thirty, I was offered a job in New Zealand, and I turned it down. I was scared, I wanted to play it safe. For years I told myself it had been the sensible choice. But in hindsight, I wish I had gone. If I had said yes, my whole life would have been completely different. I am not saying it would have been better, just different. What I have learned is this: I do not regret the risks I took. I only regret the one I did not. So when you get to that trip to Norway in November, promise me you will make the most of every single day. Take the harder road. You will never regret it. Talk soon.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the science of regret, and why it does not work the way you think. Here is the surprising part. When researchers ask older people what they regret most, they almost never mention the risks that went wrong. They mention the chances they did not take. The trip they talked themselves out of. The person they never called. The leap of faith they were too afraid to make. We imagine that playing it safe protects us, but in the long run, it is the missed opportunities that stay with us. So here is the takeaway. The next time you are standing at a fork in the road, and one path is safe and one is a little frightening, remember this. In twenty years, you are far more likely to regret the road you did not take than the one you did. If only more people understood that a little sooner.
